data.title: Tesla: AI And Robotics Shift Is The Real Story
data.description:
Earlier this month, I covered Tesla, Inc.'s (TSLA) delivery and production results for the first fiscal quarter, which are routinely released ahead of the company’s earnings report and which showed moderate growth year-over-year. Last week, Tesla beat earnings expectations for Q1 but fell slightly short on revenues amid its inventory buildout and a decline in regulatory credit revenue.

Notwithstanding, Tesla achieved its highest gross profit margins in a year and benefited from considerable growth in its free cash flow as well as an expansion in its free cash flow margins. Tesla has a ton of catalysts in its business—ranging from a shift to AI and robotics to new AI hardware investments—including a strategic pivot to SpaceX technologies and a $25.0B CapEx program focused on AI compute.

Despite a much higher-than-average P/E ratio in the Magnificent 7 industry group, I believe the risk profile remains attractive for Tesla as the company is preparing for mass production of the Optimus Humanoid robot this year, which is set to accelerate its shift towards becoming an AI and robotics enterprise.

Previous rating

Despite an increase in inventory that I previously discussed in my work—Don't Worry About Inventory—I believe that Tesla is widely underrated as an evolving robotics and AI company. While investors are still focusing primarily on the company’s achievements in the electric vehicle market, which experienced its fair share of headwinds lately, the company is retooling its factory in Freemantle to commence Optimus production. Tesla’s first-quarter earnings report card showed solid free cash flow growth, sequential FCF margin gains, and higher gross profit margins, which continue to place Tesla at the very top of the EV market in terms of profitability.

Accelerating shift towards AI/robotics investments

Tesla reported $22.39B in consolidated revenue for the first quarter, showing 16% year-over-year growth. Yet, the EV maker reported sales below the consensus expectation of $22.64B amid higher inventories in its core market.

While Tesla missed expectations on the top line for Q1'26, the company did very well in terms of its gross margins, however, aided by favorable tariff adjustments and one-time benefits related to warranties. In the most recent quarter, Tesla published a GAAP gross profit margin of 21.1% (+1 PP Q/Q), which was the highest level for the tech company in more than a year.

In my last work on Tesla, I highlighted that the tech enterprise was leading its competitor, BYD Company Limited (BYDDF), in terms of gross profit margins, and Tesla's Q1’26 report indicated that Tesla is in a good position to expand its margin lead throughout the year. The main reason for this is that Tesla announced price increases for some of its EVs—as evidenced by the $15,000 price increase on Model S and Model X units—while BYD continues to feel the pinch of a saturated Chinese market and higher competition.

In terms of free cash flow, Tesla had a strong quarter as well: the EV company generated $1.4B in free cash flow on total revenue of $22.4B, which calculates to an FCF margin of 6.5% (+0.8 PP Q/Q). Compared to the year-earlier period, which is when Tesla suffered headwinds related to a politically motivated consumer boycott for its EVs in North America and Europe, the EV company saw a considerable improvement in its FCF profile in Q1'26: Tesla's free cash flow grew 117% year-over-year… meaning its FCF grew approximately 7.3X faster than its consolidated top line.

$ in millions Q1'25 Q2'25 Q3'25 Q4'25 Q1'26 Y/Y Growth
Total revenues $19,335 $22,496 $28,095 $24,901 $22,387 16%
Net cash from operating activities $2,156 $2,540 $6,238 $3,813 $3,937 83%
Capital expenditures ($1,492) ($2,394) ($2,248) ($2,393) ($2,493) 67%
Free cash flow $664 $146 $3,990 $1,420 $1,444 117%
Free cash flow margin 3.4% 0.6% 14.2% 5.7% 6.5% 88%

Source: Author

AI and robotics shift, higher CapEx guidance, $2.0B SpaceX investment

Tesla already announced that it is winding down production of its low-volume Model S and Model X lineup, which is set to free up manufacturing capacity for the Optimus Humanoid robot. Tesla is currently retooling its factory in Fremont to prepare for mass production of its robotics line. According to Tesla, the company seeks to start production for its first mass-marketable version of the Optimus Humanoid robot (the Gen 3) in late July and August 2026.

Not only is Optimus production going into the mass production phase, but Tesla also announced that it is investing $2.0B in SpaceX, Elon Musk’s space and satellite company, ahead of the firm’s much-anticipated IPO (expected date: June 2026).

In addition, Tesla announced that it is going to invest up to $2.0B into an AI hardware company—as per the company’s Q1’26 10-Q disclosure—to bring inference-optimized chip production in-house (likely related to Optimus Gen 3 production). Further, to accelerate this shift towards becoming more focused on AI and robotics products, Tesla increased its CapEx guidance from $20.0B this year to $25.0B (a 25% raise vs. previous guidance).

Therefore, while EV sales will remain key to Tesla’s financial performance in the short and medium term, the tech company is laying tracks to gradually pivot its business towards a heavier AI focus. While the ramp for Optimus Humanoid production will likely be very slow—Tesla may only produce a few thousand units initially to ease out production flaws—Elon Musk has guided for 1 million produced units per year by the late 2020s and 10 million units annually at full global capacity (likely early 2030s).

According to Precedence Research, the robotics market is set to grow by a factor of 3.8X in the next nine years to a size of $416.3B, which calculates to an annual average growth rate of 14% until 2035. With Tesla at the forefront of humanoid production, I believe the tech enterprise continues to deserve a premium earnings multiplier.

Tesla’s valuation

Tesla’s shares are not cheap, and they have historically never been. While the tech company was expensive in the past, mainly due to the rapid expansion of the total addressable market in the EV segment, the outlook for Tesla in the future will depend more on the prospects for AI deployment and its robotics business.

In my opinion, Tesla’s shares will remain expensive when evaluated based off forward earnings, as the market is pricing in the firm's massive potential in the expanding robotics market. Tesla is currently trading at 150X forward earnings (FY 2027), which is high but nonetheless below the tech firm’s 1-year average P/E ratio of 173X. The 1-year average P/E ratio implies a fair value for Tesla of $435 per share, implying 16% upside potential.

Compared to other tech companies in the Magnificent 7 group, investors are paying a massive premium for Tesla’s growth potential. However, Tesla has also achieved the second-fastest market cap growth in the Magnificent 7 benchmark group in the past ten years: it was only outperformed by NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA). I believe Tesla's valuation premium is justified, under the condition that the company can successfully transition to Optimus production in the next several years and expand its AI/robotics portfolio.

Risks with Tesla

There are a number of risks for Tesla, which include the elimination of the federal tax incentive to buy an electric vehicle last year as well as growing price competition in the EV market more generally. Delayed Optimus mass production or robotaxi launches (expected for H2'26) could also weigh on investor sentiment going forward and could compress Tesla's valuation premium. What would get me to change my mind about Tesla is if the company suffered a decline of its gross profit margins below 20% or failed to deploy $25.0B in investment capital this year.

Closing thoughts

Tesla’s earnings scorecard for the first fiscal quarter was in many ways much better than expected, given that the company already reported an increase in its inventory, especially in its core market in North America. Tesla benefited from a considerable increase in its free cash flow—which is growing much faster than its top line—and the company managed to grow its gross profit margins in each quarter since Q1'25, highlighting margin momentum.

Most importantly, Tesla significantly boosted its CapEx guidance by 25% to $25.0B and is on track to further shift towards becoming an AI and robotics company with mass production of the Optimus robot set to commence this year. I like the setup for Tesla, and I am very well aware of the massive growth premium that investors are paying here. Considering that Tesla has always been expensive and that the transition to AI/robotics is well underway, I believe the risk profile is skewed to the upside, even at a P/E ratio of 150X.

data.title: FOMC Preview: The Powell-To-Warsh Transition Matters
data.description:
Why This FOMC Meeting Matters

The FOMC meeting in late April will likely be uneventful on interest rates; however, it will by no means be irrelevant to the financial markets.

The Federal Reserve Board is generally expected to hold the target range at 3.50%-3.75%. I believe there is little to no chance of an interest rate reduction at this time. I also don't anticipate that Chairman Powell will present a dramatic policy shift during the announcement.

That doesn't imply that investors needn't pay attention to the event. What's important isn't the rate decision; rather, what matters is how Chairman Powell describes the next steps for the Fed. Specifically, whether the Fed continues to perceive cuts as its normal course of action, or if high and persistent inflation, rising oil prices, and a fragile job market necessitate a protracted and potentially adverse pause for the Committee.

It's relevant because equities are currently trading near all-time highs, rate markets have effectively removed almost all of the anticipated easing from their pricing models earlier in the year, and credit spreads continue to be tight. In my opinion, the significance of the April FOMC is more about what the Fed chooses to disavow than what it decides to do at this particular point in time.

The Dual Mandate Dilemma

The central banking dilemma Powell faces in this April 29th meeting is one that is easy to explain in terms of its components; however, it is difficult to resolve. On paper, the Federal Reserve’s dual mandate is straightforward: control inflation and promote maximum employment. However, as we can see today, these mandates are currently working against each other.

We still have inflation that remains too high. As we know, core PCE rose again to 3.0% in February 2026. Since economists’ forecasts for Q2-2026 PCE inflation have been increased to 3.6% from 3.3% in the March surveys, consumers are growing increasingly anxious about rising prices. They now believe that prices will rise by a 4.7% annual rate for the next year.

No, inflation is not gone. Rather, it has simply taken a new form. Initially, the oil shock appears to have impacts only on rising gasoline prices: higher gas station prices, higher transportation costs, and higher headline inflation. The Federal Reserve generally views those types of increases as temporary and therefore is able to "look past" them. However, if high oil prices last long enough, they begin to affect other areas of the economy (food, goods, services, company profit margins) and ultimately consumer expectations. At that point, what was initially a short-term shock begins to become a "credibility" issue for the Federal Reserve.

Be honest, are you also remembering the story of "temporary inflation"? That famous post-Covid phase in which Powell continued to call it transitory for months before the market realized that perhaps it wasn't so transitory. I agree; every now and then those old memories come back to me too, especially in this historical period.

At the same time, Powell doesn’t have an easy reason to justify staying hawkish because of the labor market. Weekly jobless claims numbers are at 214,000 levels, which seem pretty good on paper. Employers aren't laying off employees rapidly. However, there's another side to that picture. As Governor Christopher Waller pointed out, the current state of the labor market makes it vulnerable to economic shocks. Additionally, he stated that the growth in the labor force is probably near zero.

The difference could have an impact that's bigger than you realize. If, for example, fewer workers will enter the workforce, then it won’t take as many new hires each month to keep unemployment at a very low rate. Although, at first glance, this may look like a solid labor market just by looking at the numbers, the core strength of the engine is decreasing. It is as if a car is driving along the road, but the fuel indicator is showing that there is hardly any fuel left.

An Impossible Choice Between Inflation and Employment

As shown in the minutes of the March FOMC meeting, this tension was quite evident. The vast majority of Federal Reserve participants believed that progress towards the goal of achieving a 2% inflation rate would likely be slower than anticipated.

In simpler words, most of these officials were concerned that inflation may exceed its target level for a longer period of time than previously thought. BofA also reported on an internal division among Fed officials. Specifically, the vast majority of Fed officials viewed upside risks to inflation and downside risks to employment. Furthermore, some officials were concerned that persistently high oil prices could cause inflation to remain elevated for a long time and necessitate additional interest-rate hikes if price stability became threatened.

However, there's a catch. While most of the Fed participants believed that a prolonged conflict in the Middle East could weaken the labor market further (and thus provide a justification for rate cuts), there is also evidence suggesting that the same shock that causes inflation to increase could also provide a justification for rate cuts during periods of weaker-than-expected growth and job loss.

It is also why traditional Fed speak ("data-dependent", "measured", "patient") does little to address anything. The data itself is sending conflicting signals. The inflation component is telling Powell, "Be cautious." The labor market component is saying, "Be careful." When your dashboard is flashing both red warning lights simultaneously - i.e., when you are facing conflicting information from two critical sources - it typically means you should do nothing rashly. Instead, you should steer steadily until things become clearer on the horizon.

The Energy Shock Debate: Transitory Redux or Structural Inflation Threat?

Oil prices surged to a record of $118.35 per barrel (bbl) of Brent crude on March 31, 2026. Although prices have declined from their peak levels, the current prices are sufficient to continue upward pressure on inflation.

A shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz for a quarter would increase US inflation by approximately 0.6 percent this year, according to estimates provided by the Dallas Federal Reserve. UOB Economic-Treasury Research increased its 2026 headline CPI estimate to 3.3 percent, up from 2.6 percent, and its core CPI estimate to 2.8 percent, up from 2.6 percent, due to the revised estimates caused by the crisis.

Is this a short-term, one-off surge in price increases, or is this the beginning of sustained inflationary pressures?

UOB believes that energy-driven inflation represents a one-time shock, while the weakening labor market and weaker consumer spending should occur later. Therefore, they believe that the Fed will remain on hold until April before lowering the Fed Funds Rate twice, first in June and again during the third quarter, and conclude at 3.25% by year-end. I believe that the Fed's own view will differ significantly from the views currently reflected in market pricing, which may assume the oil shock is more severe than will ultimately be viewed by the Fed.

However, those arguing in favor of the Fed taking a more aggressive stance with regard to monetary policy (particularly with regard to interest rates) are growing louder, and I believe this is something investors cannot ignore. As for me, I believe that US inflation will continue to rise in both the short and medium term due to continued pressures from energy issues originating in the Middle East. While higher oil prices certainly indicate considerable economic pressure at the gas pump, it is also possible that these prices can translate into higher transportation costs and food prices (potentially translating into higher business costs), thereby reducing the purchasing power of real household incomes. This translates into two simultaneous impacts on inflation and demand.

I don't want to be the pessimist, but I think we'll face a more restrictive environment for the Fed. By the March 2026 meeting, the Fed's Dot Plot was already reflecting higher inflation expectations and less ease with which the Fed could implement additional accommodation. Furthermore, by April, the marketplace discussion had evolved even further, moving beyond "fewer" cuts, to possibly needing to begin discussing active tightening measures if price stability is not maintained.

Furthermore, between the January and March FOMC Meetings, the number of FOMC Members indicating support for a rate hike this year increased.

Therefore, this is not merely an academic discussion about oil. Rather, it is a direct issue related to asset prices. If the Fed continues to treat the impact of oil as a temporary phenomenon, then the path to lower rates will continue to be viable, albeit likely delayed or reduced in size. Such a scenario would be beneficial to equity markets, fixed income markets, credit markets, and gold.

However, if the Fed begins to view the impact of oil as a threat to inflation expectations, then this would mark a complete reversal in market sentiment. Under such conditions, Chairman Powell would confront obstacles defending his central bank's credibility. Therefore, under these conditions, the actual policy debate will move from "When do cuts begin?" to "Are hikes back on the table?"

Therefore, oil has become so much more than simply a commodity story; it now serves as a key moment between a mundane Fed pause and a much more unpleasant policy environment. Personally, I think Powell won't be too forthcoming in his final meeting, so he'll try to maintain the exact same script as the previous meeting. That's my feeling.

A Regime Change Masquerading as Continuity

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting set for April 29, 2026, is more than your typical routine interest rate decision. To me it represents the last days of one Fed era and the first days of an entirely new one.

Investors are focused on the “rate” question. " Will Jerome Powell keep interest rates unchanged?". If so, "will he be able to give us an idea of what the future may entail for prospective cuts by the Fed?" To me, the bigger picture, though, has got to be around the transition from Powell to Kevin Warsh and what it could tell us about how the Fed reacts.

Powell's term expires on May 15th. Now that Senator Thom Tillis has cleared the path for Warsh to be confirmed by the U.S. Senate on April 26th, the likelihood that a regime change in leadership is inevitable seems increasingly high. In my view, though, markets are mostly treating this possible regime change as a minor event. Markets seem to have accounted for Warsh as just a slightly more hawkish Powell. I believe this simplifies things far too much.

Warsh is not just Powell with a slightly stronger or more serious tone. Warsh has publicly called for a "regime change," not only in the theoretical underpinnings of monetary policy but also in its real-world execution. This means something. Furthermore, I do not believe that the full implications of this position have been factored into investor sentiment.

In his Senate testimony, Warsh described a completely different approach to the role of central banking. He spoke positively of "trimmed-mean" inflation measures, which could be used as a basis for making policy decisions based upon information beyond what a simple reading of headline inflation data provides. He expressed a preference for reducing the size of the Fed's balance sheet versus relying on lower interest rates. He made clear that there was no compelling reason for him to recommend cutting interest rates, even with a big number of investors believing that lower interest rates are merely being delayed, rather than denied.

For these reasons, I perceive the transition from Powell to Warsh as representing more than simply a change in personnel. It has the potential to represent a fundamental philosophical shift within the Fed. Less emphasis is placed on providing forward guidance. A greater emphasis is placed on balancing the size of the Fed's balance sheet. Less pressure was applied to reassure investors. A potentially greater willingness to allow some level of economic hardship if such hardship is necessary to restore price stability.

It is the latter aspect that I believe investors should take most seriously. While the April FOMC meeting may result in little change in interest rates, I believe the regime change taking place behind the scenes may create significant changes in financial market conditions, valuations for various assets, and our assumptions about how the Fed responds when markets begin to falter.

Market Positioning and the Asymmetric Risk Profile

I find the biggest difference between now and the beginning of this year is how much markets have already moved. Early in 2026, investors were expecting 2.5 Fed cuts through the end of 2026. This is now an entirely different story. As we can see from the next chart, Fed Funds Futures now price less than one full rate cut this year, down from those earlier expectations of about 2.5 cuts by the end of 2026.

Therefore, yes, part of the "hawkish repricing" has occurred. The bond market is not in fantasyland anymore. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury Yield is currently trading at approximately 3.78%, up from a low of 3.60% in April 2025. The 10-Year U.S. Treasury Yield is at approximately 4.30%. Therefore, the curve has flattened. Generally, when the curve flattens, markets either expect the Fed to continue being restrictive or markets begin to worry about slower economic growth. I personally think both of these factors are showing themselves in markets.

However, looking at equities, it does not appear as consistent of a picture. The S&P 500 is currently making new all-time highs. Therefore, I believe investors remain comfortable with the concept of a "soft landing". However, I believe many investors also believe that if growth begins to falter, the Fed will ultimately favor growth vs. inflation.

This is where I see problems. Rates have adjusted. Equity markets have not adjusted nearly as much. When two markets are telling two different stories, I typically listen to them.

Goldman Sachs has issued a warning that, based on their equity asymmetry model, another equity decline is highly probable, and the potential for a rally appears limited. In my recent article, I wrote that stock markets are ignoring the high oil prices and the lack of success in diplomacy and talks between the parties.

Credit is yet another area where I would act with due diligence. Credit spreads remain tight, albeit in spite of significantly greater confusion around our overall macro environment. Tight credit spreads make me nervous. Typically, when investors are not compensated greatly for assuming credit risk, even a relatively small change in financial conditions can result in a larger-than-expected reaction. Obviously, private credit is particularly vulnerable in this regard. Private credit has already experienced redemption pressure; therefore, it is ill-suited for a rapid shift in liquidity.

2 scenarios

It is for this reason that I believe the risk/reward profile into the April 29 FOMC is asymmetric.

  • A dovish surprise from the FOMC could likely lead to a 2-3% rally in equities. That would not be surprising to me. If Powell appears to be unconcerned regarding oil, emphasizes concerns regarding the labor market, and leaves the door open for rate cuts later in the year, long-duration growth stocks should rebound.
  • On the other hand, I believe the opposite scenario would be more concerning. If Powell comes across as either more hawkish than expected, or simply states more clearly that additional rate hikes could occur if inflation continues to run hot, the market reaction could be more severe. A 4-5% decline in equities would certainly not be unreasonable in such a scenario, particularly in Nasdaq, software, unprofitable technology companies, leveraged credit products, and any product that is heavily dependent upon cheap liquidity.

I am concerned with what I perceive to be a disconnect between markets having priced in no changes to monetary policy and what I perceive to be a Federal Reserve that is willing to become less focused on preserving asset prices. And that is where Warsh becomes important.

While markets may be prepared for delayed cuts and/or extended pauses, I am not confident that markets are prepared for a Federal Reserve that places balance-sheet reductions, maintaining inflation credibility, and reduced forward guidance above preserving investor confidence in financial markets. That is an altogether new setup. If we are headed toward that type of Federal Reserve, the true repricing of assets may not come from this meeting, nor will it come quickly, but it may occur slowly.

I don’t believe we can focus solely on the April 29 meeting and whether or not Powell will make any comments. I think the biggest problem right now is how Warsh behaves post-May 15. In my opinion, based upon both Warsh’s recent Senate testimony and public commentary, the likely next Fed Chair has a completely different view of monetary policy than his predecessor. It appears at present that the general consensus among the public is that they are looking forward to a new Fed Chairman that will be slightly tougher and more restrictive, and more rule-abiding than Powell. Nothing more.

While Powell stressed data dependence and flexibility, Warsh stated that “we need regime change,” and the best way to achieve this is to “significantly reduce” the Fed’s balance sheet and implement a “rules-based anti-inflation strategy” that is much stricter than the current flexible, data-dependent framework. Warsh also publicly endorsed the use of the “trimmed mean” inflation indicator, which takes into account less volatile pricing categories, as a possible rationale for making monetary policy decisions. The trimmed mean currently reflects lower inflation rates than those reflected in the PCE index and potentially supports additional rate cuts regardless of whether total inflation continues to rise. That leaves room for interpretation.

The Warsh Wild Card: Balance Sheet Obsession and the Coming Liquidity Shock

However, paradoxically, despite stating the above, Warsh’s testimony was viewed as hawkish, and I have noted that he did not present a strong enough case for cuts and was “tougher” than I had anticipated.

The resolution to this paradox lies within Warsh’s obsession with reducing the size of the balance sheet. I believe that under a Warsh-led Fed, you will see rising long-term yields while traders prepare for potential short-term interest rate cuts, and a reduced balance sheet. This is the key insight: Warsh may allow for rate cuts or pursue them while simultaneously pursuing aggressive quantitative tightening and implementing a policy that reduces financial conditions through withdrawing liquidity as opposed to raising the policy rate.

Additionally, I want to make another important point related to both myself and markets. For individuals familiar with the concept of the “fed put” (i.e., the belief that the Federal Reserve will ease monetary policy in order to prevent destabilization of the economy), this is going to represent a major paradigm shift. Private credit, leveraged loans, and long-duration assets that were assumed to exist in a stable financial environment could face a liquidity crisis if Warsh were able to accelerate his efforts to shrink the balance sheet. Bank exposure to private credit is already a source of concern for regulators, and a liquidation crisis, as advocated by Warsh, could lead to such levels of credit distress that would necessitate action by the Fed, however only after considerable damage had been done to equity markets.

As mentioned previously, markets believe there to be continuity between Powell and Warsh regarding the Fed’s response function. However, this assumption could be proven catastrophic.

data.title: OceanFirst Financial: Rare Discount To Tangible Book Ahead Of Merger
data.description:
Today we continue our deep dive into the Q1 2026 earnings for the regional banking sector. In today’s column, we shift our focus to OceanFirst Financial (OCFC), a regional player in the New Jersey and New York metropolitan markets. With approximately $14.6 billion in assets, OceanFirst is about to see some big changes as it is going through the final stages of its merger with Flushing Financial (FFIC). Organically speaking, the bank has also been repositioning its core operations. What do we mean? Well, management has recently decided to discontinue its residential loan origination business to lean harder into its commercial and industrial strengths. Thus, this regional bank is a bit unlike most of the others in our coverage universe. Let us touch on the merger and then discuss the just-reported Q1 results.

Flushing Financial Merger

According to the merger terms, the merger is expected to be financially attractive for OceanFirst. While actual results may of course vary at the time of the announcement, management foresaw an estimated 2027 EPS accretion of approximately 16% with a tangible book value dilution of approximately 6%. It is thought this will be earned back in approximately 3 years. Further, it sees a 2027 return on average tangible common equity of approximately 13% and a return on average assets of approximately 1.00%. Margins are expected to hold up at a respectable 3.2%. As part of the deal, Flushing shareholders will receive 0.85 shares of OceanFirst stock for each Flushing share, resulting in them owning about 30% of the combined company. Post-merger completion, OceanFirst will have roughly $23 billion in assets, about $18 billion in total deposits, and $17 billion in total loans. Now let us turn to the just reported results to see how the bank is doing organically.

OceanFirst Financial's Q1 2026 Earnings Headline Performance

In the headline results, the bank reported revenue of $103.2 million, up 5.6% from the same period last year. While this was a slight miss against consensus estimates, the earnings power was strong. Q1 GAAP earnings per share came in at $0.36 and were impacted by about $4.3 million in merger-related expenses and restructuring charges. Backing out those one-time items, adjusted EPS was $0.43, a healthy beat of $0.04 over the $0.39 consensus expectations. It was also a nice increase of $0.41 in EPS reported in the sequential Q4. What about organic moves in loans and deposits?

OceanFirst Financial’s Q1 2026 Loans and Deposits

As regular readers know, we are always looking for organic growth in loans and deposits as a measure for not just the bank’s health, but of the local customers and local economies being served. Well, we saw organic growth, albeit modest, in loans. Total loans increased to $11.12 billion, representing a 3% annualized growth rate. But recall above we told you that the bank is moving more into the commercial and industrial space. Well, here the bank saw a 19% annualized boost in these loans of $105.1 million. Deposits overall remained stable at $11.16 billion. We see no signs of concern in these results ahead of the merger.

Margins Expand

Like most of the regional banks we have covered this earnings season, OceanFirst saw continued strength in its interest income power. In Q1, the net interest margin expanded to 2.93%, up from 2.87% in the sequential Q4 and up from 2.90% a year ago. As we have noted for the sector, this was because of a reduction in the cost of funds, with the total cost of deposits dropping to 1.97%. That comes even as asset yields faced some pressure from lower rates. Given that post-merger completion the bank is anticipating a 3.2% net interest margin, that would be a 27 basis point expansion from where we were in Q1, which is a solid expectation. Margins over 3% are what we look for in regional banks. The best banks we cover approach or even exceed 4%, but over 3% is strong.

OceanFirst Asset Quality is Stable

Despite the broader economic headwinds and the uptick in criticized loans we’ve seen in some of the other bank reports we have covered, OCFC’s credit quality stands out as very strong. Net charge-offs were next to nothing at just $701,000, or roughly 0.03% of average total loans. Folks, this is significantly lower than the industry average for banks. While we did note a slight uptick in nonperforming assets to 0.26%, that figure is still among the best of the banks we follow. That said, the increase was largely attributed to a single commercial relationship and is not a sign of anything systemically wrong in the areas being served, in our opinion. The allowance for credit losses stands at $89.9 million, or 0.81% of total loans, as they head into the merger closing.

The return metrics in Q1 showed a nice rebound from the sequential quarter, though they remain slightly compressed compared to the prior year. There are some merger-related expenses mixed in there. The return on average assets hit 0.57%, a solid improvement from the 0.36% seen in the sequential Q4. A better measure is the adjusted core return on average assets. Those were 0.68%, up from both 0.65% in Q4 and 0.62% a year ago. Similarly, the return on average tangible common equity bounced back to 7.22% from a recent low of 4.57% in Q4. The adjusted return on average tangible equity was a strong 8.56%. Q4 saw a poor efficiency ratio of 80.37%, but it improved to 71.13% in the current quarter. Making adjustments, the core efficiency ratio was 66.76%. While the trend improved, we do note that in our coverage universe, this is less efficient than most we cover. We like to see the gold standard of 50% on this measure, while your average bank is around 60%. However, given 2027 expectations for expansion on margins and a focus on the commercial and industrial loans, provided there are no drastic changes in rates, we suspect efficiency will improve into next year.

Final Thoughts

The upcoming merger with Flushing Financial is, of course, a clear catalyst here. The closing is expected within a few months. But folks, we have a unique situation here. At $19.08 a share, OCFC is actually trading at a slight discount to its tangible book value of $19.86. Unlike some of its peers trading at 1.5x or 2x tangible book, we find this attractive coupled with the 4%+ dividend yield. The risk here is both macro for all banks and, of course, the execution risk of a major merger. That said, with the expectation of a successful merger and the accretive nature long-term of the move, we think buying below tangible book value is worth a flyer.

data.title: Headwater Exploration: A License To Print Money
data.description:
Headwater Exploration (CDDRF) is a company that moves from very profitable (as covered in the last article) to "a license to print money" in the current environment. This is a company that proves the point that to do very well in a higher-priced environment, no financial leverage is needed when a very low-cost structure is in place to start with. So many times, investors believe that a lot of leverage will lead to big profits in this industry. But that only happens if the levered entity can decrease leverage rapidly while increasing profits rapidly. A company like Headwater Exploration can increase profits rapidly without worrying about having to delever. The result is that the cash piles up at an alarming rate, just waiting to be put to use at some more of these high-profit ideas.

Waterflood

Let's go back again and look at the idea behind the waterflood proposal that is rapidly decreasing the decline rate.

Canadian companies tend to update investors monthly. Note that this Canadian producer reports using Canadian dollars unless otherwise noted. Once you find the three-year assumptions for one slide, you have basically found the three-year assumptions for the presentation. The numbers either are not going to vary (for consistency purposes of the whole presentation) or will vary a little for specialized situations.

What happens is those payback periods contract as oil prices rise. If history is any guide, at one point during fiscal year 2022, several of these companies reported 3 payback periods in one year during the first full year of waterflood. But the company is using considerably more conservative prices in the presentation.

What this means is that even one month (as is likely) of higher prices because of the Iranian situation in the first quarter is highly likely to materially increase earnings compared to what is shown above. The longer this situation lasts, the more time this company has to "rake in the money" far in excess of many in the industry. No financial leverage needed.

Companies that are extremely profitable, like this one, pile up a lot of cash to more rapidly expand production (if they want to) and so usually do as well or better than a leveraged option of your choice. This is what many investors fail to understand about this industry and why a leveraged choice is not the bonanza that many think it will be.

Meanwhile, if commodity prices happen to disappoint, then this company merely returns to above-average profitability (while raking in the cash at a slower rate). A financially leveraged company could fail or be in dire financial straits if prices disappoint. Here, the growth story and above-average profits would merely continue at a slower rate.

Multi-Zone Benefit

It is a similar story with the multizone benefits. As commodity prices rise, then the payback period contracts accordingly.

The examples shown here fit in the category of operating leverage. The cost structure here is low enough that even if commodity prices go back down, this company has the option to reinvest the extra cash received from higher commodity prices back into the business because even at lower prices, the reinvestment makes good business sense.

The growth story is a bit more robust in the case of a low-cost producer like this one. It is more robust because the growth story makes sense at lower commodity prices. But a financially leveraged company may have to go back to diverting more cash flow to bringing the leverage down ASAP "just in case".

A side issue is that I seldom see a company that has multiple payback periods at really any commodity price level assumptions (let alone one like this one that at times in the past has received as many as 3 paybacks in the first year of production). This company is so far ahead of its competitors when it comes to multiple paybacks that I can find nothing comparable in the industry to the Clearwater Basin heavy oil business.

Basin Boundary is Still Expanding

Another key to the growth story here is that the basin boundaries for this very profitable growth story are still expanding with still more discovery wells.

So, the latest update is still an unlimited horizon because the exploration wells are still extremely successful. It is clear that the companies involved have not found the boundaries of this low-cost basin yet. The thing to realize is that it takes some time for last year's discoveries to "prove out". Therefore, continuing positive reports are essential to the original discovery story.

Furthermore, from time to time this technology is expanding to other heavy oil basins with similar success in Canada. That likely means that heavy oil production costs overall will be coming down for the foreseeable future from what they once were.

Pelican Area

Here is one of several expansion areas beyond the original Martin Hills field of operations (which is where this company began operations).

One of the keys here to the fast payback periods shown before is the ability to begin low-cost secondary recovery relatively fast. "Low-Cost" being the key words.

As long as these successes continue in the monthly updates, then this company is going to pile up a lot of cash that will likely be used to expand the original capital budget if it becomes significant. Remember that this company has long carried no long-term debt. So, there is no debt to service properly or repay.

Different Opportunity

The Martin Hills area is, in general, the furthest along in terms of secondary recovery and exploration. However, one interval may have the opportunity for intermediate-grade oil if characteristics allow for that better pricing. The API measure has barely crossed into medium-grade territory.

Understand that the oil quality here is a good deal better than the rest of the production. Now whether it is ever possible to get better pricing for this oil is another matter entirely. There may not be enough of it, relatively speaking, to make the effort worthwhile.

But it does point to the interval variability in the basin. There are still more intervals to go. Something like this points to an outside possibility of additional profitability as further exploration and development continues.

As of right now, this is just really good oil for the area.

Summary

Above-average profitability can oftentimes prove to be a superior way to make a whole lot of money without the risk that goes with financial leverage. This company is going to "rake in the money" at current prices in a way that few competitors can match.

But what is even better is that if commodity prices happen to unexpectedly decline to where they were, this company still has a lot of very profitable ideas to expand the business even at lower commodity prices.

That makes this one a strong buy idea with some downside protection against lower commodity prices. Costs here are so low that there is a possibility of reasonable cash flow during a cyclical downturn. This company would be among the first to recover during the recovery part of the business cycle because of its low costs.

I would therefore continue to hold as long as the growth story continues. In fact, I would likely hold until this management sells the company because it has built and sold companies before.

Risks

The biggest risk would be a technological advance that moves the low-cost title to another basin or another part of the upstream business. That title has switched in the past. Therefore, it is very likely to happen at some point in the future.

A cyclical downturn does not hold the fears here that it holds elsewhere. A debt-free company can simply "cash checks" until a cyclical recovery begins. The fact that costs are low enough to likely allow the "cash checks" strategy to succeed even if the heavy oil discount expands is quite a statement that was not possible for heavy oil (ever) in past business cycles.

The light oil crowd in the United States (for example) by and large does not have the low costs that are evident here even before the premium that light oil commands is considered. This has to be considered an exceptional opportunity in the upstream business. I do not find multiple paybacks (period) in the industry as a general rule using just about any assumptions I might use.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
